text: The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to lift the rectum in a cephalad manner, exposing the presacral space for the total mesorectal excision (TME). The third robotic arm is positioned to provide macroscopic traction, while the left hand with a fenestrated bipolar acts like a Parkes retractor, performing a three-dimensional scooping motion to mature the posterior dissection from midline to the left and right. The surgeon carefully manages small bleeders to preserve the integrity of the mesorectal envelope.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to lift the rectum out of the pelvis, exposing the presacral space in preparation for the total mesorectal excision (TME). The third robotic arm is positioned to provide macroscopic traction while the left hand, equipped with a fenestrated bipolar, performs precise three-dimensional movements to mature the dissection in the posterior plane. The dissection begins at the posterior midline and progresses laterally to both the left and right sides.

The surgeon creates a posterior midline defect in the mesorectal envelope and matures it to the left and right, carefully managing small bleeders using a fenestrated bipolar cautery to avoid damaging the levator plate or precyclovenous plexus.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, maturing the defect from the center to the left and right, while managing bleeding with a fenestrated bipolar cautery to avoid damaging the levator plate or precyclovenous plexus. The mesorectum is lifted off the areolar tissue plane, and lateral attachments are peeled off. The anterior peritoneal reflection is scored, and the rectovaginal septum is identified using cautery. The assistant uses suction and pulls the rectosigmoid junction out of the pelvis to maintain exposure.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, peeling off lateral attachments from the mesorectum on the left side, then repeats the process on the right side. The anterior peritoneal reflection is scored at the sulcus formed by the previous hysterectomy. The surgeon uses a vaginal dilator and an EEA sizer to identify the rectovaginal septum, while the assistant uses suction and pulls the recto-sigmoid junction out of the pelvis to maintain exposure.

The surgeon uses the assist to pull the rectum out of the pelvis, exposing the rectovaginal septum. The third arm is used to poke, spread, and lift, dissecting the anterior wall of the rectum from the posterior wall of the vagina in an avascular plane. A defect is created midline, matured to the left and right, and the lateral stocks are taken. The fellow completes the dissection by unzipping the thin lateral stocks and dividing attachments down to the pelvic floor, ensuring circumferential dissection to the levator plate. The mesorectum is mobilized for upward division at a right angle, distal to the proximal rectal cancer, using a 30mm robotic staple with a green load.

The surgeon uses the assist to pull the rectum out of the pelvis, exposing the rectovaginal septum. With the third robotic arm, the surgeon pokes, spreads, and lifts to dissect the anterior wall of the rectum from the posterior wall of the vagina in an avascular plane. A defect is created midline, then matured to the left and right. The lateral stocks are thin and filmy, and the fellow is tasked with unzipping them. The surgeon ensures circumferential dissection down to the levator plate, leaving some mesorectum due to the proximal rectal cancer, allowing for upward mobilization and division distal to the tumor.

The surgeon uses a 30mm robotic stapler with a green load to divide the rectum at a right angle, completing the division in the pelvis. The stapler is positioned carefully to fit within the confined space, demonstrating the nuanced technique required for low pelvic stapling.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) by tenting the rectum anteriorly to expose the mesorectum. The junction is identified, and careful dissection is performed to avoid entering a false plane or the periosteal layer of the pre-sacral space. The aerial tissue is separated using cautery, while the third arm remains stationary to provide stability.

The surgeon tentatively pulls the rectum anteriorly, revealing the mesorectum and nervous structures. Care is taken to avoid entering a false plane, ensuring the incision is made correctly to access the presacral space behind the rectum. The fenestrated bipolar in the left hand acts as a Parkes retractor, lifting and pushing the tissue, while the hook cautery in the right hand separates the areolar tissue. The third arm remains stationary, providing stability during the dissection.

The surgeon uses the left hand with a fenestrated bipolar instrument to lift, push, and pull towards the head, mimicking a Parkes retractor, while the right hand operates a hook cautery. The presacral space is exposed, with nerves tented up and pulled down to enter the true presacral space. The third arm remains fixed, and the assist provides suction to maintain visibility. Contouring marks are made at the anterior peritoneal reflection to guide incision placement.

The surgeon matures the precircular space and approaches the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. Using contouring marks, the surgeon scores the anterior peritoneal reflection at the peritoneal sulcus, creating trace marks for precise incision. The assist holds suction to maintain a clear surgical field during the posterior dissection.

The surgeon uses the third arm to perform anterior dissection by inserting it at the anterior midline, spreading, and lifting towards the head to fan the anterior peritoneal reflection. The assist holds suction near the camera lens to clear smoke plumes, improving visibility. The surgeon applies deep counter-traction with the third arm positioned at eleven o'clock while the left hand pulls towards five o'clock to detach remaining anterior and posterior attachments. A zero-degree scope is used for direct visualization of the presacral space and anterior prostate, enhancing optical clarity.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection while the third robotic arm is inserted at the anterior midline, spreading and lifting towards the head to fan the anterior peritoneal flexion. The assist uses suction to clear smoke plumes, improving visibility as the camera lens clears.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to apply deep counter-traction on the left anterior attachments of the disorectum, positioning the arm at eleven o'clock while the left hand pulls at five o'clock. This technique is repeated for the right anterior attachments, with the third arm at two o'clock and the left hand pulling at seven o'clock. The surgeon switches to a zero-degree scope for a direct view of the presacral space, enhancing visualization during the procedure.
